Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd block out time, like 32 hours a week sounds about right, but I wasn't always mentally present during those hours. I'd sit with my materials, read through things, take notes by hand, but there wasn't a consistent structure to it. Some weeks I'd be really on it, other weeks I'd just be going through the motions. I think being 26 and having done this for a while, I've kind of lost that freshman energy for organized study schedules.
Q2: It definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I have extracurriculars that take up time, and sometimes those felt more engaging than coursework, if I'm being honest. When assignments were due, I'd buckle down, but otherwise my studying was more reactive than proactive. I'd tell myself I'd study at the same time each day, but that rarely actually happened consistently.
Q3: Usually a deadline or a notification about something due would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I was missing or if grades had been posted. It wasn't really about wanting to learn more—it was more about making sure I wasn't falling behind. I'd also log in when I felt guilty about not having checked in for a while, which happened more often than I'd like to admit.
Q4: For a brief period mid-semester, yeah, logging in became somewhat routine. I think it happened because I was juggling multiple online courses and needed to keep track of everything in one place. But that routine faded pretty quickly once I realized I could download most materials and work offline. The LMS became more of a checkpoint than a daily habit for me.
Q5: The reading materials and any written resources were most useful to me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and articles and work through them with a highlighter and my own notes. The videos were okay too—I watched a lot of them—but I retained more from text-based content. Discussion boards weren't really my thing; I only posted once all semester.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion boards after my one required post. They felt performative, like people were just posting to get participation points rather than having genuine conversations. I also delayed watching some videos until right before exams because I knew I could skim through them quickly. If something felt like busywork, I'd put it off or skip it entirely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd engage with it. But if something felt like it was just there to pad the course content or check a box, I'd lose interest fast. At this point in my academic career, I'm pretty pragmatic about where I invest my energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore. Required ones got done because they had to get done—that's pretty much the only difference. I rarely did optional stuff unless I was genuinely curious, which wasn't often this semester. There's something about the word 'optional' that my brain translates to 'skip this.'
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download everything I could and work from printed materials or saved files. I have good resources at home, so I'd spread out my notes on my desk and study without needing to be connected. A lot of my learning happened with a notebook and pen, not clicking through the LMS.
Q10: There were entire study sessions where I was working through problems, re-reading chapters, and making flashcards, but none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd spend hours with downloaded lecture slides, annotating them by hand. The LMS would just show that I logged in once to grab the files, but the actual work happened completely offline over several days.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once, download everything, and then study from those files for weeks without going back to the LMS. It was more convenient for me since I don't have reliable internet access all the time. One download could fuel a whole week of studying.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. I was stressed, moderately anxious, and juggling too many online courses at once. My attendance dropped during that period because I just couldn't bring myself to engage with everything. The CBT session I had helped me process some of that, which was honestly surprising—I didn't expect it to make a difference, but it did help me reframe some of my stress.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars and just life stuff meant the LMS often got the bare minimum from me. Confusion about assignment requirements also made me avoid certain tasks longer than I should have.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while doing other things, or I'd scroll through readings without actually processing them. It was like I was physically present in the LMS but mentally checked out. Those sessions felt like going through the motions to ease my guilt about not studying.
Q15: I postponed studying for exams way too long, even knowing they were important. I think it's because the exam content felt overwhelming and I kept telling myself I'd understand it better if I waited. Avoidance became a coping mechanism for my anxiety around performance. The irony is that waiting just made the stress worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without a deadline, I wouldn't engage with the LMS at all. I'd see something was due, calculate how much time I had, and then cram accordingly. It's not a healthy pattern, but it's how I've operated for years now. The LMS became a deadline tracker more than a learning tool.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, but honestly, by the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. If feedback was positive, I'd feel briefly good but not change anything. If it was critical, I'd feel defensive and then eventually try to apply it to the next assignment. The delay between submission and feedback made it feel disconnected from my learning process.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard, which meant I knew how to navigate it but didn't feel particularly inspired by it. Having five online courses meant everything blurred together. The structure didn't push me to engage more deeply—it just gave me a checklist to work through. More variety or interactive elements might have helped.
Q19: The fact that I could download most materials made engagement easier in some ways because I could study on my own terms. But the discussion requirements felt forced and made me less likely to participate genuinely. Also, having so much content spread across modules made it hard to see the big picture, which was demotivating.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos passively, but not actually converting that activity into completed work. High activity doesn't mean high focus. Someone could spend hours in the LMS and still be mentally elsewhere, especially if they're stressed or anxious.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like my situation. My exam scores weren't great, but I passed, and a lot of my learning happened outside the LMS. If you just looked at my login frequency, you'd think I wasn't trying, but I was putting in hours with downloaded materials. The LMS doesn't capture offline effort at all.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you logged in, what you clicked, how long you stayed. But they miss everything qualitative: whether you were actually thinking, whether you understood the material, whether you were stressed or just going through the motions. They capture behavior but not cognition or emotion.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just about showing up or completing tasks—it's about being curious and invested. Honestly, I wasn't fully engaged this semester. I did what I needed to pass, but I wasn't intellectually present the way I should have been.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my offline study sessions, my anxiety about exams, or how the CBT session actually helped me cope better toward the end of the semester. It wouldn't show that I was surprised by how much I learned despite feeling disengaged, or that my extracurriculars sometimes taught me more than my courses did. The human stuff gets lost in the data.
